diagrams.description: This timeline traces the key milestones in the development of Sociology. It begins with Auguste Comte coining the term in 1838 and establishing positivism. The 1848 Communist Manifesto by Marx and Engels laid groundwork for conflict theory. The late 19th century saw Émile Durkheim's seminal works on social facts, methodology, and suicide (1893, 1895, 1897). Max Weber introduced interpretive sociology with The Protestant Ethic (1904–05). The 1920s Chicago School advanced urban sociology and symbolic interactionism. Mid‑20th century brought structural functionalism (Parsons, 1930s‑40s) and a revived conflict perspective (Mills, Coser, 1950s‑60s). From the 1970s onward, feminist sociology, critical race theory, postmodernism, and globalization expanded the field, leading to digital sociology in the 2010s.

timeline title History of Sociology section Origins 1838 : Auguste Comte
coins "sociology" (Positivism) 1848 : Marx & Engels
Communist Manifesto section Classical Foundations 1893 : Durkheim
The Division of Labor in Society 1895 : Durkheim
Rules of Sociological Method 1897 : Durkheim
Suicide (empirical sociology) 1904-05 : Max Weber
The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism section Early 20th Century 1920s : Chicago School
Urban sociology & symbolic interactionism 1930s-40s : Talcott Parsons
Structural functionalism section Mid‑Century Developments 1950s-60s : Conflict theory revival
(C. Wright Mills, Lewis Coser) section Late 20th Century & Beyond 1970s : Feminist sociology
Critical race theory 1980s : Postmodernism
Globalization studies 2010s : Digital sociology
& computational social science
